insights.insight: The ad industry pays $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution that infers purchase intent. A cryptographically verified receipt eliminates the inference step entirely — the conversion event is the signal, not a proxy for it.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and device fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the industry has no cross-ecosystem deterministic signal at scale. First-party data solves this only within a single company's walled garden.

Verified receipts are settling at roughly $0.10 per confirmed purchase event against an industry CPA floor of $5. That 50–200x cost gap is structural, not cyclical — it reflects the elimination of attribution infrastructure, not a discount on it.

The dataset has diffused into rural and secondary markets across the Northeast where probabilistic targeting has the weakest signal quality. Verified purchase data from underserved geographies carries a scarcity premium that urban-concentrated panels don't replicate.

A Stripe receipt, Apple receipt, and Google Play receipt all resolve to the same spend token regardless of payment rail or platform. This cuts across the silo problem that limits every major platform's purchase data to its own ecosystem.

insights.topic: Developer infrastructure spend as a receipt category
insights.insight: Five pending vendor approvals — Notion, Render, Vercel, DigitalOcean, Hetzner — target SaaS and cloud infrastructure, a category with predictable subscription cadence and high average order value. Approval expands a queryable, identity-free dataset in a segment most ad tech panels underrepresent.
